findings.title: Unsupervised CSM epoch-selection procedure is absent; epochs are hardcoded
findings.claim: The number of training epochs — the paper's 'only data-dependent hyperparameter' — is returned from a per-dataset if/elif table of hardcoded constants (47 branches, values 1..5000); no code computes the Improved Contrast Score Margin (CSM) criterion T(f) the paper says was used to choose these epochs.
findings.concern: Appendix B.6 claims epochs were chosen by an automated unsupervised CSM tuning method (Li et al. 2025b), but that selection code is not in the repo, so the headline TCCM numbers cannot be reproduced from scratch and the per-dataset epoch values have no verifiable provenance.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the CSM epoch-selection script, the candidate-epoch search ranges per dataset, and the exact data partition on which T(f) was evaluated.
findings.paper_ref: Appendix B.6 'Unsupervised Epoch Selection Strategy'; B.3
findings.reason: Quote confirmed at lines 73-78 of FMAD/functions.py; if/elif chain with no CSM computation verified; repo-wide grep finds no CSM criterion code outside baseline files.

findings.title: No code for the Friedman/Nemenyi tests and critical-difference diagrams (Figs 21, 22)
findings.quote:
We report the results using critical difference diagrams (see Figure 21 and 22). For AUPRC, the
Nemenyi test indicates that there are no statistically significant differences among the top-performing
group, which includes TCCM (ranked 5.8), DTE-NonParametric, LUNAR, KDE, AutoEncoder, ICL,
findings.claim: The paper performs Friedman + Nemenyi post-hoc significance testing and reports critical-difference diagrams (Figs 21/22) for its main ranking conclusions (App D.5), but a repo-wide grep finds no Friedman/Nemenyi/critical-difference/post-hoc code in any non-baseline script.
findings.concern: The statistical-significance result the NeurIPS checklist (item 7) points to as supporting the main conclusion has no producing code, so the significance claim cannot be reproduced from the artefact.
findings.resolution: Authors: please provide the script that runs the Friedman/Nemenyi tests and renders the critical-difference diagrams from the aggregated rank CSVs.
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt line 4812; repo-wide grep for friedman/nemenyi/critical-difference finds no matching code in non-baseline scripts.

findings.title: Feature-level explainability (Fig 4 MNIST; App D.4.2 attribution validation) has no code
findings.quote:
As shown in Figure 4, the model highlights the
additional horizontal stroke that distinguishes 7 from 1, demonstrating that the learned importance
scores align well with human-interpretable cues.
findings.claim: Explainability is one of the five headline claims (the title's 'Explainable'); the paper shows a MNIST 1-vs-7 feature-attribution figure (Fig 4) and a controlled synthetic attribution-validation study (App D.4.2), but no script in the repo computes or visualizes per-feature residual attributions, the MNIST setup, or the synthetic validation.
findings.concern: A central contribution of the paper has no reproducible artefact: nothing in the code produces the feature-wise importance maps or quantitatively validates them, so the explainability claim is unverifiable from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the explainability scripts (MNIST 1-vs-7 attribution figure and the App D.4.2 synthetic attribution-accuracy experiment).
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt line 1128; repo-wide grep for attribution/explainability/feature-importance finds no matching code in non-baseline scripts.

findings.title: Ablations for Figs 16 (standardization), 17 (time-weighted sampling), 18 (AE+TE) absent
findings.quote:
results = run_time_embedding_ablation()
plot_time_embedding_ablation(results)
# ======================================================== #
# ======================================================== #
"""
Ablation Study 3: Training with or without Noise
"""
noise_results = run_noise_ablation(data_configs, seed_list)
plot_noise_ablation(noise_results)
# ======================================================== #
# ======================================================== #
"""
Ablation Study 4: Contamination Ratio Sensitivity (Per-Dataset Max)
"""
results, contamination_levels_dict= run_training_contamination_ablation_dynamic_fixed_split()
findings.claim: AblationStudies.py only produces Figs 12, 13, 14 and the contamination figure; the appendix also reports Fig 16 (effect of z-score standardization), Fig 17 (effect of interpolated time-dependent inputs z_t=tz), and Fig 18 (TCCM vs AE+TimeEmbedding), none of which have code (repo grep for 'standardiz'/'interpolat'/'AE+TE'/Figure_16-18 finds nothing in non-baseline files).
findings.concern: Three appendix ablations that justify TCCM design choices (normalization, no time-interpolation, residual learning vs AE) cannot be reproduced because their experiment code is not in the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the scripts producing Figures 16, 17, and 18.
findings.reason: Code quote confirmed at AblationStudies.py lines 594-609; grep for standardiz/Figure_16-18 in non-baseline scripts finds no code producing those three ablation figures.

findings.title: No code for Fig 1 (synthetic illustration), Fig 6 (GMM mismatch), or §C.3 collapse check
findings.quote:
Figure 6 shows boxplots
of the score distributions for normals and anomalies across d ∈{2, 5, 10, 15, 20}. In all cases, anomalous points exhibit consistently higher scores than normal points, with AUROC values exceeding
0.9 regardless of dimension.
findings.claim: Figure 1 (ring/two-moons/blobs vector-field visualization), Figure 6 (boxplots validating the anomaly-mismatch assumption of Props 3-5 over d in {2,5,10,15,20}), and the §C.3 representation-collapse tracking are described as empirical validations, but no script produces them (RobustnessStudy.py generates GMM data only for the PGD attack curves, not these figures).
findings.concern: The empirical evidence backing the theoretical propositions and the conceptual illustration is not reproducible from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the scripts for the synthetic illustration (Fig 1), the mismatch-validation boxplots (Fig 6), and the collapse analysis (§C.3).
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt lines 3366-3368; grep for ring/two-moons/blobs/collapse finds no script producing Fig 1, Fig 6 boxplots, or §C.3 collapse analysis.

findings.title: run_knn.sh invokes non-existent Full_experiments.py (actual file FullExperiments.py)
findings.claim: run_knn.sh calls `python Full_experiments.py` (underscore + lowercase 'e'), but the only matching file at the repo root is `FullExperiments.py`; the four invocations in this script will fail with 'can't open file'.
findings.concern: The KNN driver script crashes on every dataset because of the filename typo; it is not used by the README's main roadmap, so impact is limited, but the script is non-functional as shipped.
findings.resolution: Rename the invocation to `FullExperiments.py` (the other bash scripts already use the correct name).
findings.reason: Quote confirmed at bash_files/run_knn.sh lines 27-28; `Full_experiments.py` does not exist in repo root while `FullExperiments.py` does, confirming the typo.

findings.title: determine_FMAD_hyperparameters has no else branch; unknown dataset -> undefined locals
findings.claim: The function is a long if/elif chain with no final `else`; if a dataset name matches no branch, `epoch_size`/`batch_size`/`learning_rate` are never assigned and the `return` raises UnboundLocalError. All 47 shipped datasets do resolve (verified), so it does not affect the paper, but any new/renamed dataset crashes.
findings.concern: A reviewer running TCCM on a dataset whose name is not in the table gets an opaque UnboundLocalError instead of a sensible default; the substring matching is also collision-prone for future dataset names.
findings.resolution: Add an `else` branch with default epochs/batch/lr (and prefer exact-name matching over substring `in`).
findings.reason: Quote confirmed at FMAD/functions.py lines 259-268; function ends at line 268 with return immediately after the last elif and no else branch, confirming UnboundLocalError risk for unrecognized datasets.

findings.title: requirements.txt pins torch==1.13.1 but paper states PyTorch 2.0
findings.claim: requirements.txt pins `torch==1.13.1`, while Appendix B.3 states the implementation 'is based on Python 3.9.21 with PyTorch 2.0'.
findings.concern: The pinned environment does not match the reported one; for this MLP it is unlikely to change results, but it is a reproducibility-environment inconsistency.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm which PyTorch version produced the reported numbers and align requirements.txt with the paper.
findings.reason: requirements.txt line 7 confirmed as `torch==1.13.1`; paper_text.txt line 2686 confirmed paper states 'PyTorch 2.0', corroborating the mismatch.

findings.title: Epoch-selection criterion needs anomalies, but only test data contains them — possible test tuning
findings.quote:
where ˆµO, ˆσ2
O denote the mean and variance of anomaly scores for the top-k predicted anomalies, and
ˆµI, ˆσ2
I correspond to the remaining n −k presumed inliers. For each candidate epoch, we compute
T(f) and select the configuration maximizing this criterion.
findings.claim: The CSM epoch-selection criterion T(f) is computed over a set partitioned into 'top-k predicted anomalies' vs 'presumed inliers'. In the semi-supervised setup the training set is normal-only (utils.py:34 `X_normal`), so a set containing anomalies is the *test* set; selecting epochs to maximize the anomaly/inlier margin on test outputs would tune the model on the test set.
findings.concern: If the per-dataset epoch counts (the only data-dependent hyperparameter, which strongly affects scores) were selected by maximizing T(f) on test-set anomaly scores, the main AUROC/AUPRC results would be optimistically biased by test-set tuning; the absence of any held-out validation split in the code (utils.py only makes train/test) makes this impossible to rule out.
findings.resolution: Authors: state explicitly which data the CSM criterion was evaluated on. If it used the test split, re-select epochs on a held-out subset of training normals (or a labeled validation fold) and report the impact on the headline numbers.
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt lines 2780-2783; utils.py lines 30-48 confirm training uses only X_normal and only a train/test split exists with no validation set, corroborating the structural concern.
